data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-08T00:08:30.918Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about .NET/EF Core breaking changes. Let me provide what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, there was a significant change regarding synchronous operations. The Cosmos DB provider has always been inherently asynchronous (since the underlying Cosmos SDK is async-only). In earlier versions, EF Core allowed "sync-over-async" — wrapping async calls in synchronous wrappers — but this was opt-in and could cause deadlocks.


Starting with EF Core 9, the Cosmos provider began throwing exceptions for synchronous operations by default, requiring explicit opt-in. By .NET 11 / EF Core 11, that opt-in is being removed entirely, so all synchronous LINQ terminal operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, and `Count()` will no longer work and must be converted to their async equivalents.

I should be clear though—my training data doesn't cover .NET 11 specifics since it releases in November 2026, so I'm projecting based on the established pattern of deprecation rather than confirmed knowledge. is that in .NET 11, the opt-in mechanism for synchronous Cosmos operations is completely removed, so any sync calls like `ToList()`, `First()`, or `SaveChanges()` will throw `InvalidOperationException` with no workaround available—the only path forward is converting to async equivalents. I should note I'm not entirely certain about .NET 11's specifics since it hasn't released yet, but this aligns with the clear trajectory EF Core has been following with the Cosmos provider.
